draft.introduction: Confessions Book 10 is Augustine’s exploration of memory as the vast palace of the mind, culminating in the paradox of seeking God whom one must already know in order to seek. It contains the famous cry: “Late have I loved you, beauty so ancient and so new.”

translation.text: Late have I loved you, beauty so ancient and so new, late have I loved you! And behold, you were within and I was without, and there I sought you. You were with me, but I was not with you. Those things held me far from you, which would have no being unless they were in you. You called and cried aloud, and broke open my deafness.
translation.original_text: Sero te amavi, pulchritudo tam antiqua et tam nova, sero te amavi!

translator_notes.note: The sero te amavi passage reverses the Neoplatonic ascent: God is not found by climbing upward but by discovering what was always already present within. Augustine’s use of all five senses—you called, shone, breathed fragrance, gave taste, touched—makes this a synesthetic theology of grace.
